title: Respiratory physiology: Lung volumes
body_html:
The diagram below demonstrates the lung volumes which may be measured:

Click on image to enlarge

Tidal volume (TV)
  • volume inspired or expired with each breath at rest
  • Average around 500ml

Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV) = 2-3 L
  • maximum volume of air that can be inspired at the end of a normal tidal inspiration
  • inspiratory capacity = TV + IRV

Expiratory reserve volume (ERV) = 700-1100ml
  • maximum volume of air that can be expired at the end of a normal tidal expiration

Residual volume (RV) = 1.2L
  • volume of air remaining after maximal expiration
  • increases with age
  • RV = FRC - ERV

Functional residual capacity (FRC) = 1.8-2.4L
  • volume of air remaining in the lungs after a normal tidal expiration
  • FRC = ERV + RV

Vital capacity (VC) = 3-5L
  • Maximum volume of air that can be expired after a maximal inspiration
  • Average around 5L in males and 3-3.5L in females
  • Decreases with age
  • VC = IC + ERV

Total lung capacity (TLC) is the sum of the vital capacity + residual volume

Physiological dead space (VD)
  • Dead space is the air in the lungs that does not take part in gas exchange
  • VD = tidal volume * (PaCO2 - PeCO2) / PaCO2
  • where PeCO2 = expired air CO2

Summary of lung volumes and capacities
Lung volume/capacityAverage volume maleAverage volume femaleCalculation
Vital capacity (VC)5000ml3000mlIRV + TV + ERV
Inspiratory capacity (IC)3800ml2400mlIRV + TV
Functional residual capacity (FRC)2400ml1800mlERV + RV
Total lung capacity (TLC)6000ml4200mlIRV + TV + ERV + RV
Inspiratory reserve volume (IRV)3300ml1900ml
Tidal volume (TV)500ml500ml
Expiratory reserve volume (ERV)1100ml700ml
Residual volume (RV)1200ml1100ml


Spirometry cannot be used to measure residual volume (RV) and therefore cannot be used to obtain values for FRC or TLC as these measurements include the RV.

Measurement of the residual volume has to be done via indirect methods such as radiographic planimetry, body plethysmography, closed circuit dilution (including the helium dilution technique) and nitrogen washout.

Ageing and emphysema
Ageing has effects on lung volumes primarily as a result of changes in pulmonary elastic and resistive properties.

Total lung capacity (TLC) does not change significantly with age but the composition of lung volumes change with advancing age as follows:
  • Functional residual capacity (FRC) ↑ increased
  • Residual volume (RV) ↑ increased
  • Inspiratory capacity (IC) ↓ decreased
  • Vital capacity (VC) ↓ decreased

Loss of alveolar wall structural support, muscle atrophy and other changes associated with advancing age result in a reduction in lung elasticity, increased chest wall compliance and increased air trapping. This is sometimes termed 'senile emphysema'

In emphysema increases in FRC and RV are also seen however TLC will increase slightly in patients with emphysema.

Obstructive disease
In obstructive lung disease the following is seen:
  • Functional residual capacity (FRC) ↑ increased
  • Residual volume (RV) ↑ increased
  • Total lung capacity (TLC) ↑ increased
  • Inspiratory capacity (IC) ↓ decreased
